model_narrative: We run a small manufacturing operation that makes a handful of products over a sequence of periods. Each product is built from raw materials that we start with in stock and draw down as we produce. In every period we decide how much of each product to make, and we track how much of each raw material remains on hand. We want to choose the production plan that earns us the most total profit, where profit is the income from what we make over the horizon, reduced by the cost of storing raw materials and adjusted by whatever value is left in the raw materials we still hold at the very end.

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the products that can be manufactured

sets.name: r
sets.doc: the raw materials consumed by production

sets.name: tl
sets.doc: the extended sequence of periods, numbered consecutively starting at one; this runs one period longer than the production horizon so that the leftover stock at the very end can be valued

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the production periods, numbered consecutively starting at one; these are the periods in which manufacturing actually takes place, a subset of the extended sequence with the final extended period left out

params.name: b
params.doc: the initial on-hand stock of each raw material at the start of the first period, in units

params.name: d
params.doc: the storage cost charged per unit of a raw material held into a production period, in dollars per unit per period

params.name: f
params.doc: the residual unit value of each raw material left over at the very end of the horizon, in dollars per unit; may be negative when leftover stock is a liability to dispose of

params.name: m
params.doc: the maximum total production output allowed in any single production period, in units

params.name: a
params.doc: the amount of a raw material consumed to manufacture one unit of a product, in units of raw material per unit of product

params.name: c
params.doc: the unit profit earned from a product in a given production period, in dollars per unit

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the production level of each product in each period of the extended sequence

vars.name: s
vars.doc: the stock of each raw material on hand at the beginning of each period of the extended sequence

vars.name: profit
vars.doc: the total income earned over the horizon net of storage cost, plus the residual value of leftover stock

constraints.name: x[p,
constraints.intent: Total output in any production period is capped. For each production period, the combined production of all products in that period must not exceed the maximum allowed output for a period.

constraints.name: s[r,
constraints.intent: The amount of each raw material on hand has to be tracked period by period as production draws it down. At the very start of the sequence, the stock of each raw material equals its given initial stock. In every later period, the stock of a raw material at the beginning of that period equals what was on hand at the beginning of the previous period, less everything consumed by the production carried out in that previous period, where consumption depends on how much of each product was made and how much of the raw material each product uses.

constraints.name: profit
constraints.intent: Total profit has to be defined so the objective can be driven by it. Profit is the income earned from production across the production periods, valuing each product by its per-period unit profit and how much of it was made. From this, subtract the storage cost charged on the raw material held at the beginning of each production period, and add the residual value of the raw material stock remaining at the start of the final extra period beyond the production horizon. Set the total profit equal to this combined amount.

query: The current plan concentrates output on one or two products per period — bolts in period 2, washers in period 4, and that's basically it. Shouldn't a more diversified mix still hold up on profit? What's keeping the model from requiring that no single product makes up more than 60% of total production within any given period?

query: Production swings sharply between periods — period 2 is busy, periods 1 and 3 are nearly empty. Couldn't we require that the total production in any period stays within 30 units of the previous period's total? Shouldn't a smoother schedule still be at least competitive on profit?
expected_pyomo:
def smooth_up_rule(model, t):
    if t == 1: return Constraint.Skip
    return sum(model.x[p, t] for p in model.p) - sum(model.x[p, t-1] for p in model.p) <= 30
def smooth_dn_rule(model, t):
    if t == 1: return Constraint.Skip
    return sum(model.x[p, t-1] for p in model.p) - sum(model.x[p, t] for p in model.p) <= 30
model.smooth_up = Constraint(model.t, rule=smooth_up_rule)
model.smooth_dn = Constraint(model.t, rule=smooth_dn_rule)

query: Two of our three products barely get made — the plan leans almost entirely on bolts. Shouldn't every product earn at least a minimal place in the schedule, say at least 5 units each across the horizon? What's stopping the model from keeping all three lines alive like that?

query: Most periods sit nearly idle while period 2 carries the whole plan. Couldn't we keep every period working — at least 10 units of total output in each one? Shouldn't a steadier baseline still pull a workable profit, and what's keeping the model from spreading the load out?
